definition: RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) and MAE (Mean Absolute Error) are regression evaluation metrics that summarize average prediction error on held-out data. RMSE = √((1/n) Σ (yᵢ − ŷᵢ)²); MAE = (1/n) Σ |yᵢ − ŷᵢ|. This topic is the canonical home for interpreting, comparing, and reporting these metrics after training.
layman: After a model predicts numbers (prices, temperatures, demand), RMSE and MAE tell you how far off it was on average. MAE is the typical miss in the same units as the target — off by $5k on average, for example. RMSE also uses those units but punishes big misses more heavily because it squares errors before averaging and taking the square root.
content:
## RMSE and MAE as evaluation metrics

In **regression**, the model outputs a continuous **ŷ** for each true **y**. After training, you score predictions on **held-out** data (validation or test) with **evaluation metrics** — numbers stakeholders and researchers use to compare models.

**RMSE** and **MAE** are the two most common regression metrics. Their core formulas match **MSE** and **MAE** from **Loss Functions** — but the **role** differs:

| Role | When | Canonical topic |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Training loss** | Optimized during fit (gradient descent) | **Loss Functions** (MSE, MAE) |
| **Evaluation metric** | Reported on val/test; not directly optimized | **RMSE & MAE** (this topic) |

Do not re-derive MSE/MAE as training objectives here — see **Loss Functions** for per-example **(y − ŷ)²** and **|y − ŷ|** as losses and when to train with each.

### Formulas (batch over n examples)

**MSE** (mean squared error):

**MSE = (1/n) Σᵢ (yᵢ − ŷᵢ)²**

**RMSE** (root mean squared error):

**RMSE = √MSE = √((1/n) Σᵢ (yᵢ − ŷᵢ)²)**

**MAE** (mean absolute error):

**MAE = (1/n) Σᵢ |yᵢ − ŷᵢ|**

Both RMSE and MAE share **the same units as y** (dollars, °C, seconds). MSE is in squared units and is usually reported only as an intermediate step toward RMSE.

### Worked example

True **y = [10, 12, 8]**, predictions **ŷ = [9, 15, 8.5]**:

| i | yᵢ | ŷᵢ | residual eᵢ | eᵢ² | \|eᵢ\| |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 10 | 9 | −1 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 12 | 15 | 3 | 9 | 3 |
| 3 | 8 | 8.5 | 0.5 | 0.25 | 0.5 |

- **MSE** = (1 + 9 + 0.25) / 3 = **10.25 / 3 ≈ 3.42**
- **RMSE** = √3.42 ≈ **1.85** (same units as y)
- **MAE** = (1 + 3 + 0.5) / 3 = **4.5 / 3 = 1.5**

The large miss at index 2 (error 3) pulls RMSE above MAE because squaring makes it dominate the average.

### RMSE vs MAE — what each emphasizes

| Metric | Penalty on error e | Outlier sensitivity | Typical use |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **RMSE** | e² then √average | Higher — large errors dominate MSE | Default reporting; aligns with MSE-trained models |
| **MAE** | \|e\| | Lower — linear per unit error | Skewed targets, heavy tails, interpretable "typical" miss |

**RMSE ≥ MAE** always (Jensen / √(mean of squares) ≥ mean of absolute values). Equality holds only when all residuals have the same magnitude.

### Connection to expectation and variance

Treat each residual **eᵢ = yᵢ − ŷᵢ** as a draw from an error distribution. Then:

- **MSE** estimates **E[e²]** — the mean squared residual (related to **Var(e)** when **E[e] ≈ 0**; see **Expectation & Variance**).
- **MAE** estimates **E[\|e\|]** — the mean absolute residual.

**RMSE** is the square root of an estimated mean squared error — analogous to **standard deviation** σ = √Var(X) sharing units with X.

### Loss vs metric in practice

You may **train** with MSE (or MAE) and **report** RMSE (or MAE) on the test set:

- Train with **MSE** → report **test RMSE = √(test MSE)**.
- Train with **MAE** → report **test MAE** (same formula as training loss on held-out data).

Minimizing MSE minimizes RMSE (monotonic transform). The **numbers** differ by √ but **ranking** of models by MSE equals ranking by RMSE.

### Where metrics are computed

Metrics are computed on data **not used for training** — validation for model selection, test for final reporting. See **Train/Val/Test Splits** for split discipline. **Linear Regression** and other regressors are often judged by RMSE/MAE on the test split after fitting on train.

### When to report which

```
Default stakeholder report     →  RMSE (if trained with MSE) or MAE (if trained with MAE)
Outlier-heavy or skewed y        →  Prefer MAE for interpretation
Need sensitivity to large errors →  RMSE reflects that by design
Compare to published benchmarks  →  Use the same metric the benchmark uses
```

### Summary map

- **MSE / MAE as losses** — **Loss Functions** (link; do not duplicate training treatment).
- **E[e²], E[\|e\|], units** — **Expectation & Variance**.
- **Fitting ŷ = f(x)** — **Linear Regression** and other regression topics.
- **Which split to score** — **Train/Val/Test Splits**.
keyPoints: RMSE = √(average squared residual); MAE = average absolute residual — both in the same units as y.
This topic owns evaluation and reporting on held-out data; MSE/MAE as training losses live in Loss Functions.
RMSE penalizes large errors more than MAE; RMSE ≥ MAE always, with equality only when all |residuals| match.
Train with MSE → report test RMSE; train with MAE → report test MAE. Rankings by MSE equal rankings by RMSE.
MSE estimates E[e²]; MAE estimates E[|e|] — tied to expectation and variance of residuals.
